coredata.dc:title: Amino acid-like “Edge” and nanozyme “Core” structured CDs for overcoming cellular uptake barrier and enhancing tumor penetration

coredata.dc:description: Recently, protein biologics have shown unique advantage in tumor treatment, but they include limitations like cell membrane barrier and poor tumor penetration. To overcome these predicaments, a novel type of red light carbon dots (CDs) nanozyme (Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs) was designed, featuring an amino acid-like “edge” and a specialized FeN4-coordinated nanocatalytic “core”. Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs and glucose oxidase (GOx) was used to form cascaded catalytic nanosystem FG to enhance reactive oxygen species (ROS) production efficiency. Meanwhile, the edge structure of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs exhibited strong affinity for the amino acid transporters ASCT2/LAT1, which were highly expressed in tumor cells. As a result, the transmembrane transport efficiency of GOx was significantly improved following the formation of FG. Then, hyaluronic acid (HA) was used to coat FG to obtain FG@HA, enabling controllable catalytic activity and longer circulation time. Encouragingly, in vitro/vivo results demonstrated that FG could effectively enhance the GOx transmembrane transport. Moreover, FG@HA exhibited controlled depolymerization with enhanced tumor penetration and excellent cascaded enzymatic/photothermal combined therapeutic effects with fluorescent tracing. Importantly, this study innovatively disassembled CDs nanozyme into characteristic “edge” and catalytic “core” to further understand the interaction with cells and catalytic mechanism, which provided additional support for guiding the CDs nanozyme design at molecular level.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Aberration-corrected transmission electron microscopy (AC-TEM) HAADF images (grayscale and multicolour) of a single Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs. (B) The model diagram of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs. (C) Electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) analysis of C, Fe, N, and O elements. (D-E) EELS spectra of different regions. AC-TEM of (F) characteristic edge (ADF and HAADF images) and (G-H) catalytic core (HAADF images).

(A) Schematic and hypothetical steps of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs synthesis. The red dotted circles indicate the dehydration. (B) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra of Fe(III)-CDs. (C) Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum and (D) 13C NMR spectrum. (E) UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectra of ninhydrin reaction. (F) X-ray absorption near edge structure (XANES) spectra of Fe K-edge in Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs. (G) Fourier transform of Fe K-edge extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) spectra. (H) k2-weighted Fourier transforms EXAFS spectra. (I) The corresponding EXAFS fitting curves of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs at R space. (J) Wavelet transforms (WT) contour plots of the k3-weighted EXAFS data.

TEM images of (A) FG and (B) FG@HA. (C) UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectra of nanoparticles (NPs). (D) Fluorescence emission spectra and the photographs (insets). (E) Photothermal heating curve. (F) TEM images of the NPs under different treatment conditions. (G) pH change curves and solution photograph (insets) after the addition of methyl redindicator in glucose solution (n = 3). (H) TMB absorbance change curves and photographs (insets) (n = 3). (I) SOD-like activity (n = 3). (J) GSH-OXD-like activity (n = 3). (K) Enzymatic cascade of catalytic activity (n = 3). (L) Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrum. (M) “Core” structural representation. The (N) POD-like and (O) SOD-like catalytic mechanism calculated by density functional theory (DFT). * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01 and *** p < 0.001.

Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images of the (A) uptake of NPs and (B) cell membrane labeling by 4T1 cells. (C) CLSM images of the uptake of NPs by L02 cells. (D) Western Blot (WB) analysis. (E) Molecular docking results of the “edge” structure and the ASCT2/LAT1 amino acid transporter. In the 2D images, green dashed lines indicate hydrogen bonds and red semicircles indicate non-bonded contacts. In the 3D images, yellow dashed lines indicate the hydrogen bonds. (F) Uptake of NPs by 4T1 cells with inhibitor and (G) semi-quantitative fluorescence results (n = 3). * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01 and *** p < 0.001.

(A) Lysosomal escape of FG at 2 h. (B) Lysosomal escape of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs at different incubation times (2 h, 4 h and 6 h). (C) Permeation and fluorescence analysis of NPs in tumor spheres. (D) The schematic diagram of FG enhanced permeation.

(A) CLSM images of intracellular ROS levels and (B) GSH depleting capacity at the cellular level. (C) Viability of 4T1 and L02 cells under different treatment conditions (n = 3). (D) JC-1 staining of 4T1 cells treated with different groups. (E) FCM analysis of apoptosis based on Annexin V-FITC apoptosis kit for 4T1 cells. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01 and *** p < 0.001.

(A) In vivo fluorescence images of NPs distribution and (C) mean fluorescence intensities analysis (n = 3). (B) Fluorescence images of main organs and tumor and (D) mean fluorescence intensities analysis (n = 3). (E) The tumor photograph at the end of the antitumor study (n = 5). (F) Tumor growth curves (n = 5). (G) hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), DCF, and TUNEL staining tumor after various treatments. (H) The penetration of NPs in tumors. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01 and *** p < 0.001.
(A) Preparation procedures of FG@HA nanoplatform. (B) Schematic illustration of enhanced cellular uptake and apoptosis mechanisms.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, protein biologics have shown unique advantage in tumor treatment, but they include limitations like cell membrane barrier and poor tumor penetration. To overcome these predicaments, a novel type of red light carbon dots (CDs) nanozyme (Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs) was designed, featuring an amino acid-like “edge” and a specialized FeN4-coordinated nanocatalytic “core”. Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs and glucose oxidase (GOx) was used to form cascaded catalytic nanosystem FG to enhance reactive oxygen species (ROS) production efficiency. Meanwhile, the edge structure of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs exhibited strong affinity for the amino acid transporters ASCT2/LAT1, which were highly expressed in tumor cells. As a result, the transmembrane transport efficiency of GOx was significantly improved following the formation of FG. Then, hyaluronic acid (HA) was used to coat FG to obtain FG@HA, enabling controllable catalytic activity and longer circulation time. Encouragingly, in vitro/vivo results demonstrated that FG could effectively enhance the GOx transmembrane transport. Moreover, FG@HA exhibited controlled depolymerization with enhanced tumor penetration and excellent cascaded enzymatic/photothermal combined therapeutic effects with fluorescent tracing. Importantly, this study innovatively disassembled CDs nanozyme into characteristic “edge” and catalytic “core” to further understand the interaction with cells and catalytic mechanism, which provided additional support for guiding the CDs nanozyme design at molecular level.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the main undertaker of life activities, protein is widely involved in cell proliferation, cell metabolism, cell signaling, immune response and other activities [1–3]. Therefore, several specialized proteins have been used as drug candidates for tumor therapy. Due to the inevitable severe damage of the healthy tissue by traditional tumors treatments (such as radiation therapy, chemotherapy and surgery), protein biologics therapies with fewer side effects have become one of the key ways of treating tumors [1,4]. Compared with traditional chemotherapy, protein biologics (such as enzymes, antibodies and cytokines) have unique advantages. First, the special spatial structure of protein can enable them to induce tumor cell apoptosis directly through clear signaling pathways, or indirectly regulate the tumor microenvironment/stimulate the immune response. Second, due to the high efficiency and specificity of protein biologics, their half-inhibitory concentration is usually significantly lower than that of small molecule chemical drugs and has a lower toxic effect on normal tissue [5–7]. However, delivering protein biologics with pharmacological activity to specific tumor tissues/cells faces many challenges, such as the inability to penetrate the cell membrane, unstable systemic circulation, immunogenicity and short half-life. At the same time, due to the high interstitial pressure and dense extracellular matrix within the tumor, it is particularly difficult for proteins to penetrate [8,9]. The problems mentioned above limits the therapeutic efficacy of protein biologics and also reduces patient compliance.

As a common solution for drug delivery issues, nano-carriers have attracted widespread attention from researchers due to their targeting capabilities and controlled release advantages [10]. Currently, there have been some reported carriers that can enhance the delivery efficiency of proteins, such as liposomes, polymersomes, and mesoporous materials [8,10]. Despite the advancements in these carrier systems, several significant challenges remain. The chemical synthesis of these nano-carriers could be complex and may involve multiple steps, which can limit scalability and reproducibility. Cationic materials can effectively enhance the delivery efficiency of proteins; however, they face challenges such as rapid clearance from the body and potential biotoxicity. Meanwhile, the process of loading proteins into these carriers can sometimes lead to a decrease in protein bioactivity, thereby compromising their therapeutic efficacy. Furthermore, many existing carrier materials struggle with low loading capacities, which can restrict the amount of therapeutic protein that can be delivered [8]. An ideal protein delivery carrier should form a stable complex with the protein without affecting its activity and have a high loading capacity and delivery efficiency.

As a novel carbon nano-luminescent material, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted widespread attention due to their obvious advantages in drug delivery [11,12]: 1. Ultra-small size for easy tumor penetration. 2. Photoluminescence ability for tracing. 3. Some CDs have special nanozyme active sites, which can be used for oxidation/antioxidant therapy. 4. Easily modifiable surface functional groups. 5. Good photothermal conversion ability. In recent years, there have been a few reports elaborating that CDs could enhance cell uptake of proteins and we also found this phenomenon in the previous study, but the specific mechanism is unclear [13–15]. This has led to an insurmountable dilemma: it is extremely difficult to understand and guide the efficient delivery of proteins using CDs at the molecular level.

Photothermal therapy (PTT) is a minimally invasive tumor treatment technology which uses near-infrared (NIR) light to irradiate the tumor site and cause local high temperature by the photothermal conversion material. Since tumor cells are less tolerant of heat than normal cells, the high temperature induced by PTT can effectively kill tumor cells while greatly reducing side effects [16,17]. Currently, PTT, combined with other tumor treatment strategies, is widely welcomed due to its ability to remove the shortcomings of single treatment modalities [18]. Nanozyme, referring to the nanomaterials, has special active sites and exhibits enzyme-like catalytic activity. Enzyme plays a crucial role in life processes, ensuring precise cellular signaling and efficient material metabolism. The introduction of nanozyme can alter the material regulation pathways and exhibit unique oxidation/antioxidant functions [19,20]. As an important active substance, ROS can not only inhibit bacteria, but also promote the apoptosis of tumor cells by enhancing the oxidative stress in tumor sites [21].

In this study, citric acid, urea and EDTA-Fe3+ were used as precursors to synthesize iron-doped red light CDs (Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs). The precursors interacted through dehydration to give the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs “edge” with the amino acid-like structure and nanozyme catalytic “core” with FeN4 coordination structure. This edge structure exhibited high affinity for amino acid transporters LAT1 and ASCT2 that highly expressed in tumor cells. Meanwhile, the active center “core” structure showed excellent POD-like, SOD-like, and GSH-OXD-like activity. To increase the efficiency of ROS generation, GOx was combined with the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs to form a cascaded catalytic nanosystem FG. Then, HA was used to coat FG to obtain FG@HA, which allowed the catalytic activity to be controlled and reduce clearance. The coating of HA enabled the system to achieve responsive disassembly under the catalytic action of HAase and enhance thermal motion under photo-thermal stimulation. Further experiments showed that FG@HA could meet the in vivo fluorescence tracking need and achieve complete disassembly to generate ample ROS under the stimulation of HAase/NIR. Significantly, due to the edge-existing amino acid-like structure of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs, GOx, which was previously difficult to traverse the membrane, could achieve efficient trans-membrane transport through amino acid transporter LAT1/ASCT2 after forming FG. Furthermore, the FG@HA in the tumor site could disassemble and expose FG after NIR irradiation, generating rich ROS while enhancing the effective penetration of GOx in the tumor site and achieving excellent synergistic anti-tumor effects.

Through the analysis of the fluorescence properties, enzyme-like activity and photothermal properties of CDs doped with different metal ions, red light CDs (Fe(III)-CDs) doped with Fe3+ were finally selected as the final prescription (Fig. S1, S2). Citric acid, urea and EDTA-Fe3+ were mixed uniformly in formic acid and then subjected to solvothermal treatment to obtain Fe-doped red light carbon dots (Fe(III)-CDs) (Scheme 1 . A). The TEM image of Fe(III)-CDs showed a uniform distribution with an average particle size of 3.5 nm (Fig. S3 and S4) and the HRTEM image showed lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm. Then, the morphology and structure of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs were further investigated using aberration-corrected transmission electron microscopy (AC-TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A, the grayscale and multicolour images of a single Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs revealed a spherical-like shape and regular atom arrangement, with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite carbon, suggesting the presence of a graphite-like structural feature. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) results indicated that the height of individual Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs was around 3 nm (Fig. S5). Based on the above results, it could be inferred that Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs were likely composed of multiple layers of graphene-like structure stacked together to form ultra-small spherical-like particles (Fig. 1B). Electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) analysis revealed the coexistence of C, Fe, N, and O elements in Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs (Fig. 1C), with the uniform distribution of Fe. The characteristic edge structure of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs often played a crucial role in their physicochemical properties [12]. Further investigation into the edge structure of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs, including low-energy and high-energy loss spectroscopy, showed that as the selected area moved from R1 to R5 (Fig. 1D, E and Fig. S6), approached the interior of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs, the C plasmon peak at 27 eV and the K-absorption edge at 285–290 eV continuously intensified, indicating an increasing degree of graphitization. Subsequently, ADF and HAADF images of the edge of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs were obtained (Fig. 1F), where the bright spots represent C–C/C-N units [22–24]. A graphite-like arrangement was observed at the edge of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs, with the exception of the outermost 3–4 atomic layers, which may be attributed to free carbochain/functional groups. These results suggested that the edge of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs was composed of free functional groups on the outermost layer and the inward-extending graphite-like structure.

Metallic atoms, served as active sites, can endow nanomaterials with excellent catalytic activity [25]. HAADF-STEM analysis of the distribution of Fe atoms revealed that the bright regions in Fig. 1G were like Fe atoms, which were observed to be distributed in a single-atom state [26]. It has been reported that single-atom nanozymes exhibited several advantages over other types of nanozymes, including high atomic utilization efficiency, well-defined atomic coordination and electronic configurations [27]. To further investigate the structure of the active sites, atomic-resolution images around the Fe atoms were obtained. In Fig. 1H, most of the atoms exhibited regular circular bright spots, which might be due to the regular C/N atoms arrangement in Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs. Notably, a few atoms showed elliptical bright spots, consistent with the elliptical high-intensity regions in the multicolour image, which were considered to be the distribution sites of Fe atoms. Additionally, Fe atoms were observed to be fixed in the framework in a double-pentagonal ring coordination with surrounding atoms. In summary, the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs were ultra-small spherical-like particles composed of characteristic edge structure and a single-atom Fe catalytic core, formed by the stacking of multiple graphene-like layers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical composition and the characteristic edge
ce:section.ce:para: XPS was used to determine the main surface elements of Fe(III)-CDs (Fig. 2 B) with C (59.3 %), O (25.82 %), N (13.94 %) and Fe (1.04 %), where the C1s spectrum of C=O (288.08 eV), the N1s spectrum of C-N=C (399.48 eV), N–H (401.28 eV), and the O1s spectrum of C=O (531.08 eV) and O–H (532.98 eV) (Fig. S7-9). At the same time, FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2C) indicated distinct absorption peaks at 3410, 3155, 1631, 1595, and 1186 cm−1, which could be attributed to the presence of O–H, N–H, C=O, C=N, and C-N groups. In the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. 2D), peaks at 181.25, 178.48, 159.33, and 96.76 ppm were associated with O-C=O, N-C=O, C=N, and C=C, respectively.

Subsequently, the classic ninhydrin reaction was used to investigate the presence of amino acid-like structure of Fe(III)-CDs[28]. Ninhydrin could react with the amino acid structure and produce blue-purple products with a maximum absorption peak at 570 nm. Therefore, the new absorption peak at 570 nm (Fig. 2E) suggested the presence of free amino acid-like structure at the edges of Fe(III)-CDs. Based on the structural information obtained and AC-TEM images of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs, it is inferred that Fe(III)-CDs formed “edge” with amino acid characteristics through the following steps (Fig. 2A): 1) Under solvothermal conditions, six molecules of citric acid and six molecules of urea react, losing 21 water molecules through dehydration and forming a nitrogen-doped graphenic rigid carbon framework. 2) The iminyl groups at the edge of the carbon framework formed stable intramolecular hydrogen bonds with carbonyl groups, subsequently allowing formic acid to attack the iminyl groups, resulting in the formation of free α-carboxyl and amino groups. 3) This edge structure acted as a substructure that could expand the carbon framework through further dehydration interactions, ultimately resulting in the formation of edge structure with amino acid characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe atomic coordination environment and the catalytic core
ce:section.ce:para: The doping of the metal elements could significantly affect the activity of the nanozymes [25]. Therefore, the atomic structure of Fe(III)-CDs was characterized. The chemical coordination environment of Fe in Fe(III)-CDs was analyzed using characteristic X-ray absorption spectroscopy, including both X-ray absorption near edge structure (XANES) and extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS). Reference samples included Fe2O3, FeO, Fe3O4, Fe-foil and iron phthalocyanine (FePc). As shown in the Fig. 2F, the XANES curve of the Fe K-edge in Fe(III)-CDs revealed an absorption energy situated between that of FeO and Fe2O3. This suggested that the valence state of Fe in Fe(III)-CDs was between +2 and +3, a result consistent with the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Fe (Fig. S10). The pre-edge peak at 7113 eV confirmed that this material contained a planar FeN4 structure serving as a single-atom support framework [29].

The Fourier-transformed (FT) k3-weighted EXAFS profile (Fig. 2G) for Fe in Fe(III)-CDs detected a strong peak around 1.58 Å, attributed to the FeN4 coordination at the first shell scattering paths. Meanwhile, no significant Fe-Fe peak was observed at 2.2 Å. These results indicated that Fe was atomically dispersed within the Fe(III)-CDs framework. The k2-weighted EXAFS oscillation spectra (Fig. 2H) showed that Fe(III)-CDs exhibited amplitude and periodicity similar to those of FePc, suggesting a comparable structure. Using EXAFS fitting curves, the structural parameters of the Fe sites in Fe(III)-CDs were quantitatively (Fig. 2I, Fig. S11). The results demonstrated that the chemical environment of Fe(III)-CDs corresponded to typical Fe-N single atom, with a coordination number of 3.98 and an bond length of 1.99 Å (Table. S1). This result was consistent with the observation of the structure around the Fe atoms in AC-TEM images (Fig. 1H).

Subsequently, the wavelet (WT) analysis of the Fe K-edge EXAFS oscillations was conducted assess the spatial resolution in both k and R spaces (Fig. 2J). Excitingly, Fe(III)-CDs and FePc showed maximum intensity at 5 Å−1 with the most similar distribution possibly due to the notable FeN4 coordination [30]. In summary, Fe in Fe(III)-CDs exhibited structure coordinated by four N. Based on this structural information, it was inferred that Fe(III)-CDs were synthesized with the FeN4 coordination “core” through the following steps (Fig. 2A): 1) An EDTA-Fe3+ molecule underwent cleavage of Fe-O coordination bonds under solvothermal conditions (formic acid). 2) Four molecules of citric acid and six molecules of urea lost 15 water molecules through dehydration and formed an active core with FeN4 coordination.

GOx can consume glucose to produce H2O2, while the POD-like activity of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs can convert H2O2 into highly reactive ·OH. To enhance ROS production and effectively deliver GOx, Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs were combined with GOx to obtain the composite FG. The TEM image showed that FG had a uniform distribution with an average particle size of 30.8 nm (Fig. 3 A, Fig. S12). Upon magnification, distinct black CDs were clearly observed within FG. Subsequently, HA was coated onto the surface of FG to produce FG@HA (62.4 % loading rate of GOx). Dynamic light scattering was used to investigate the hydration size and the zeta potential. The results showed that the systems were electronegative and their hydration size were slightly larger than that of TEM observations, which might be attributed to the increase of hydration layer (Fig. S14, S15). The HA coating minimized in vivo clearance while endowing the system with the capability for HAase/NIR stimulated disassembly. The TEM image of FG@HA is shown in Fig. 3B, where the system appeared nearly spherical (average particle size = 72.6 nm) as shown in Fig. S13. The UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectra (Fig. 3C) revealed a new absorption at 276 nm for FG, which might be attributed to the characteristic absorption of GOx. The above results illustrated the successful preparation of FG and FG@HA.

The excellent fluorescent properties can provide a foundation for in vivo tracking of the drug. The fluorescence properties of the NPs are shown in Fig. 3D, observing that Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs, FG, and FG@HA had similar red fluorescence emission capabilities (Em: 630 nm, Ex: 557 nm). Compared to blue/green light, red light has a longer wavelength, which can effectively reduce interference from biological tissue backgrounds, thereby enhancing imaging capabilities [31]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of FG and FG@HA was significantly higher than Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs. This might be attributed to the fact that GOx effectively prevent water molecules from interacting with the C=O groups on the surface of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs. This interaction maintained a surface electron-withdrawing environment with reduced energy dissipation, thus leading to effective red light emission in an aqueous solution [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro photothermal and stimulus-responsive depolymerization
ce:section.ce:para: Due to their intolerance to heat, tumor cells can suffer permanent damage when the local temperature exceeds 42 °C. The photothermal heating capacity of the NPs was investigated (Fig. 3E), revealing that the system could increase by 14 °C within 5 min under NIR irradiation. Subsequently, the photothermal conversion efficiency of FG@HA was calculated to be 15.3 % with well photothermal stability (Fig. S16, S17), providing a prerequisite for effective PTT. Given the coating of HA and the enhanced molecular thermal motion caused by photothermal heating, the disassembly of FG@HA under HAase/NIR stimulation was examined (Fig. 3F). The TEM images showed that untreated FG@HA appeared as uniform spheres. After NIR treatment, a slight reduction in particle size was observed, likely due to the increased molecular thermal motion causing partial NPs rupture. The HAase-treated NPs showed partial aggregation, possibly because incomplete hydrolysis of HA by HAase led to aggregation. Meanwhile, the HAase + NIR co-treated group exhibited the most significant change in particle size, mostly distributed around 38 nm (Fig. S18), close to the size of FG, indicating complete disassembly of FG@HA.

FG, composed of the nanozyme Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs and the natural enzyme GOx, was crucial for studying enzyme activity. GOx can consume glucose and O2 to produce gluconic acid, and the GOx activity was examined through pH and dissolved oxygen changes. The pH trend of the FG group was almost identical to free GOx (Fig. 3G), suggesting that Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs in FG did not interfere with GOx's activity. After NIR irradiation, the pH value of the FG group decreased significantly, possibly related to increased GOx activity at elevated temperatures within a certain range (Fig. S21). After HA coating, the rate of pH change was significantly reduced. Meanwhile, the FG@HA + HAase + NIR group showed a more pronounced trend than the FG@HA group, likely due to the disassembly of the system under HAase/NIR stimulation, exposing GOx active sites. These results were consistent with the changes in dissolved oxygen (Fig. S19).

Subsequently, the POD-like activity of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs was investigated (Fig. 3H, Fig. S20). TMB can serve as a catalytic substrate for POD-like enzymes, producing blue TMBox. To avoid interference from TMBox absorbance, 50 °C water bath was used to simulate NIR irradiation. The FG group showed no significant difference in trend from the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs group, indicating that GOx in FG did not interfere with the POD-like activity of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs. After treatment in a 50 °C water bath, the FG +50 °C group exhibited the strongest absorbance changes, possibly due to enhanced POD-like activity of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs at elevated temperatures (Fig. S22). The HA coating reduced the reaction rate, but the rate significantly increased after HAase/50 °C treatment, likely related to system disassembly.

O2 ·- can be consumed by the SOD-like activity, leading to the production of H2O2. Subsequently, the total SOD activity detection kit was utilized to assess the SOD-like activity of the NPs. As shown in Fig. 3I, the inhibition rate trends for FG and Fe(III)-CDs were nearly identical, indicating that they both possess similar SOD-like activity and the formation of FG did not affect the active sites. Finally, the SOD-like activity of Fe(III)-CDs was calculated to be 1496.3 U/mg. GSH-OXD-like can catalyze the conversion of glutathione (GSH) to oxidized glutathione (GSSG), and DTNB was used to evaluate the GSH consumption capacity. As demonstrated in Fig. 3J, both FG and Fe(III)-CDs could deplete 5 mM GSH to below 3 mM within 24 h, which aids in enhancing oxidative stress at tumor sites and promoting apoptosis of tumor cells.

GOx can consume glucose to produce H2O2, while the POD-like activity of Fe(III)-CDs can consume H2O2 to generate ·OH, resulting in the blue production of TMBox. Glucose solution containing TMB was utilized to investigate whether a cascade catalytic reaction could occur with FG. The results, shown in Fig. 3K, indicated that neither GOx nor Fe(III)-CDs alone could cause the solution to turn blue. However, the FG group effectively catalyzed TMB, producing the blue TMBox. This demonstrated that a cascade catalytic reaction could occur between GOx and Fe(III)-CDs. After HA coating, the reaction rate of the FG@HA group was significantly weakened, but the rate markedly increased following HAase/50 °C treatment, likely due to system disassembly that exposed active sites.

Next, the mechanism of TMB discoloration was investigated, and electron spin resonance (ESR) was employed to examine the types of ROS generated in the system (Fig. 3L). In the glucose solution, the FG group exhibited a characteristic peak ratio of 1:2:2:1, suggesting the production of highly oxidative ·OH within the system. Meanwhile, the FG@HA + HAase + NIR group also displayed characteristic peaks indicating a rich generation of ·OH.

In previous experiments, the active center structure of FeN4 coordination was validated, and the formation process of “core” active center was hypothesized due to dehydration among the precursors (Fig. 3M). To further prove the rationality of this active “core” structure and explore the catalytic mechanism of the nanozyme, DFT calculations [33,34] were performed to assess the excellent POD-like and SOD-like activities of Fe(III)-CDs. By optimizing the active center “core” structure, stable configurations of the reactants, intermediates and products were obtained. In the catalytic process of the POD-like enzyme of Fe(III)-CDs (Fig. 3N), H2O2 first adsorbed onto the Fe atom. Subsequently, the activated H2O2 molecule dissociated homogeneously, causing one hydroxyl group to attach to the Fe atom while the other hydroxyl group dissociated, triggering the formation of ·OH. The hydroxyl group adsorbed on the Fe atom then combined with H+ under acidic conditions to form a water molecule. After the water molecule dissociated, the active center “core” returned to its initial state. The free energy changes for various stages of the catalytic process of POD-like activity are illustrated in Fig. 3N. It could be observed that the free energy for each stage decreased, implying that the POD-like catalytic activity mediated by this “core” structure was a spontaneous exothermic reaction, with a total free energy change of −2.03 eV.

Meanwhile, during the SOD-like enzyme catalytic process of Fe(III)-CDs (Fig. 3O), O2 ·- adsorbed on the Fe atom and underwent electron transfer, the produced O2 then desorption, altering the oxidation state of the Fe atom. Following this, ·OOH adsorbed onto the Fe atom and combined with H+ to form H2O2. After dissociation, the active center “core” reverted to its original state. The free energy associated with different stages of the SOD-like catalytic process also decreased incrementally, indicating that the SOD-like catalytic activity mediated by the “core” structure was a spontaneous exothermic reaction with a total free energy change of −0.98 eV. The DFT computational results further confirmed the validity of the proposed active center “core” structure, corresponding with the excellent POD-like and SOD-like activities exhibited by Fe(III)-CDs in experiments.

The effective uptake of proteins by cells has become a severe challenge due to their fragile spatial structure, high molecular weight and electronegativity. The ability of nano-carriers to be efficiently internalized by cells is key to becoming an ideal protein delivery system. Fluorescein isothiocyanate isomer I (FITC) was used to label GOx, and CLSM was used to observe cellular uptake. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the red fluorescence in the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs group exhibited an irregular punctate distribution within the cells. The green fluorescence in the GOx group was predominantly located around the cell periphery, indicating that GOx was hardly internalized by the cells. Meanwhile, the physical mixture of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs + GOx group showed fluorescence distributions similar to the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs and GOx groups, respectively. Remarkably, after the formation of the FG complex, the distribution of fluorescence within the cells changed significantly. Both red and green fluorescence were more uniformly distributed throughout the cytoplasm with higher fluorescence intensity (4.46-fold improvement (Fig. S25)), which demonstrated the formation of the FG complex significantly improved cellular uptake of the nanocarrier. Furthermore, co-localization results revealed that both red and green fluorescence were observed even in the cell nucleus, which might be related to the size of FG being smaller than the nuclear pore[35], thus allowing the NPs to enter the nucleus. The coating with HA did not significantly reduce cellular uptake of the NPs, likely due to the affinity between HA and the CD44 receptors that were overexpressed in 4T1 cells. After NIR irradiation, there was a marked increase in red/green fluorescence intensity in the FG@HA + NIR group, likely attributed to the disassembly of the system, releasing smaller-sized FG and enhancing cellular uptake due to photothermal effects. To further investigate the cellular uptake of GOx and FG, a membrane probe (purple) was used to label the cell membrane (Fig. 4B). In the GOx group, the green fluorescence exhibited a nearly identical distribution to the purple fluorescence of the cell membrane, indicating that GOx was primarily located around the cell membrane and had difficulty being efficiently internalized by the cells. In contrast, after GOx formed the FG complex with Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs, the green fluorescence of GOx crossed the cell membrane and was uniformly distributed within the cells, suggesting that the 4T1 cells showed high-efficiency uptake for FG. Subsequently, L02 cells were used to simulate the normal cells in relation to NPs uptake. The results, as shown in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence intensity of all groups was lower, demonstrating that L02 cells exhibited weak uptake for the NPs.

The significant differences in NPs uptake between tumor cells and normal cells sparked our widespread interest. It has been reported that to sustain rapid proliferation, tumor cells often reprogram their metabolic pathways, leading to a significant increase in their demand for amino acids and resulting in the overexpression of various amino acid transporters [36]. Among these, ASCT2 and LAT1, as classic amino acid transporters, have been shown to be overexpressed in multiple tumor cell types [37]. Therefore, WB analysis was conducted to examine the expression levels of amino acid transporters in 4T1 and L02 cells. As shown in Fig. 4D and Fig. S26, the levels of ASCT2/LAT1 in 4T1 cells were significantly higher than L02 cells (3.2 and 4.2 times, respectively). Based on these results, we hypothesized that the special amino acid-like structure presented at the “edge” of the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs exhibited a high affinity for amino acid transporters, resulting in a vast difference in the uptake behavior of NPs between tumor and normal cells. To validate this hypothesis, molecular docking was performed to analyze the binding interactions between the edge structure of the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs and the amino acid transporters ASCT2/LAT1. As illustrated in Fig. 4E, multiple hydrogen bonds and non-covalent interactions (including electrostatic and van der Waals’ forces) were formed between the edge structure and the amino acid residues of ASCT2/LAT1 transporters. Notably, the amino acid-like regions of the “edge” provided more hydrogen bond contacts, and the binding energies of the edge structure with ASCT2 and LAT1 were both −7.7 kcal/mol, indicating a strong binding affinity (＜−7.0 kcal/mol). To verify the fact that FG achieved efficient transmembrane transport through ASCT2/LAT1 amino acid transporters, ASCT2/LAT1 inhibitors were added during the cellular uptake experiments[38]. The CLSM images and semi-quantitative fluorescence results, shown in Fig. 4F and 4G, revealed that the addition of the inhibitors significantly reduced the uptake of FG by 4T1 cells. At the same time, free Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs were also inhibited by inhibitors, while GOx did not (Fig. S27). This indicated that the efficient uptake of FG primarily relied on ASCT2/LAT1 transporters and was consistent with the results of the molecular docking calculations. The specific uptake of NPs by tumor cells provided a foundation for enhancing targeting and safety.

In the cellular uptake experiments, the red fluorescence from the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs group was observed to display an irregular, punctate distribution within the cells. In contrast, the red fluorescence in the FG group was more evenly distributed throughout the cells. Based on this phenomenon, we speculated that the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs were concentrated in the lysosomes after being taken up by the 4T1 cells. Meanwhile, FG acted as a cascading catalytic system that could generate abundant ·OH to quickly disrupt the lysosomes, facilitating the escape of the NPs. To validate this hypothesis, the green fluorescent probe for lysosomes was used, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 A. In the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs group, the red and green fluorescence exhibited a similar distribution and produced yellow co-localization, suggesting that the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs accumulated in the lysosomes. Differently, in the FG group, the intensity of green fluorescence weakened, while red fluorescence was more uniformly distributed, indicating that the lysosomes had been effectively disrupted, allowing the NPs to escape. Since the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs possessed POD-like activity and could generate ·OH, the co-incubation time of the cells with the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs was extended to investigate lysosomal escape (Fig. 5B). At the 2 h, the strong yellow co-localization was observed between the red fluorescence of the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs and the green fluorescence of the lysosomes. As the co-incubation time was extended (4 h and 6 h), this co-localization effect gradually diminished, indicating that lysosomal escape of the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs occurred with longer co-incubation time. In previous experiments, it was demonstrated that the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs had a high affinity for ASCT2/LAT1 amino acid transporters. However, transporters typically mediate the transport of small molecules and do not involve deformation of the cell membrane or the formation of endosomes[39]. Based on the experimental results showing that Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs accumulated in lysosomes, we proposed that their large molecular weight prevented them from being directly transported via transporters. Instead, the transmembrane transport of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs might occur due to their high affinity for ASCT2/LAT1, which enhanced interaction with the cell membrane and triggered endocytosis. Subsequently, endocytosis inhibitors were added [40], as shown in Fig. S28. The endocytic uptake pathway of FG was similar to that of the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs, being clathrin-mediated, which suggested that the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs altered the original endocytic pathway of GOx.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Penetration of the NPs in multicellular tumor spheroid (MTS)
ce:section.ce:para: To overcome the limitations of 2D cell, 3D MTS models were used to investigate the permeability of the NPs. After co-incubation with the NPs for 2 h, the CLSM with a Z-stack tomoscan model was employed to image the MTS (Fig. 5C). In the same scanning layer, the green fluorescence of the FG group was significantly enhanced compared to the GOx group, suggesting that the formation of the FG complex significantly increased the penetration of GOx within the MTS. Interestingly, the red fluorescence of the FG group penetrated deeper than that of the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs group. This might be related to FG's ability to more rapidly break through lysosomes, allowing the NPs to disperse throughout the cells. Among them, the NPs in the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) and Golgi apparatus sites would be repackaged into the vesicle to generate continuous transcytosis (Fig. 5D) [41]. This enabled the FG to transport between cells and enhanced their penetrating capacity. The permeability of the FG@HA group was weaker than FG group, which might be attributed to the increase in particle size after HA coating, obscuring the edge structure of the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs. After NIR irradiation, the red and green fluorescence intensity of the FG@HA + NIR group significantly increased, potentially related to the deaggregation of the system and the photothermal enhancement of the molecular thermal motion. Meanwhile, fluorescence intensity analysis of the 70 μm scanning layer showed that both FG and FG@HA + NIR groups exhibited stronger fluorescence intensity. These results suggested that the formation of the FG complex and FG@HA + NIR could significantly enhance the penetration of GOx within the MTS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro ROS production and apoptosis
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of NPs to generate ROS within cells is directly correlated with their cytotoxicity. 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) could react with intracellular ROS to produce green fluorescent DCF, which was observed using CLSM (Fig. 6 A, Fig. S29). The green fluorescence in the FG group was significantly higher than that of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs and GOx group, possibly due to the cascade catalytic reaction of FG, which greatly enhanced the production efficiency of ·OH. Simultaneously, the green fluorescence in the FG@HA group significantly increased under NIR irradiation, which might be related to the enhancement of cellular uptake and catalytic rate due to photothermal effects. The intracellular GSH depletion capacity of FG@HA was further investigated, and naphthalene-2,3-dicarboxaldehyde (NDA) was used as a sulfhydryl tracking reagent to label GSH in the cell. As shown in Fig. 6B and Fig. S30, compared to the control group, the green fluorescence of the FG@HA group did not change significantly, but it significantly decreased under NIR irradiation, possibly due to the depolymerization of the system and exposure of active sites. Subsequently, the MTT assay was used to evaluate cell viability. As shown in Fig. 6C, at the GOx concentration of 0.1 μg mL−1, the 4T1 cell viability in the FG@HA group was 21.5 %, significantly reduced to 9.1 % after NIR irradiation. Meanwhile, at the same GOx concentration, the L02 cell viability in the FG@HA and FG@HA + NIR groups was 88.2 % and 73.6 %, respectively, possibly due to the low affinity of normal cells for HA and low sensitivity to ROS[42]. The IC50 value for L02 cells in the FG@HA + NIR group was 0.391 μg mL−1, significantly higher than that for 4T1 cells (0.071 μg mL−1), indicating lower toxicity to normal cells and higher biosafety of the system. Since FG could rapidly break lysosomes and disperse throughout the cell, and the abundant ·OH might damage mitochondria, the JC-1 fluorescent probe was used to evaluate mitochondrial integrity. Specifically, a high mitochondrial membrane potential leads to the formation of red fluorescent JC-1 polymers in the matrix, while a low mitochondrial membrane potential causes green fluorescent JC-1 monomers to diffuse throughout the cell. The green fluorescence in the FG group was significantly stronger than Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs and GOx groups (Fig. 6D), possibly due to FG's ability which could quickly disrupt lysosomes to escape. In the FG@HA + NIR group, almost no red fluorescence was observed, and the strongest green fluorescence was shown, implying that the FG@HA + NIR combination therapy group could cause stronger mitochondrial damage. Severe mitochondrial damage often induces apoptosis, so Annexin V-FITC/PI double staining was used to detect NPs-induced apoptosis. The results, as shown in Fig. 6E, indicated that the FG group could cause more severe apoptosis (73.6 %) in 4T1 cells compared to Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs and GOx. Meanwhile, FG@HA + NIR group showed the highest apoptosis rate (95.6 %). In summary, FG, due to its stronger ·OH generation capability, could produce abundant ROS within cells and induce apoptosis. At the same time, due to the enhanced cascade catalytic reaction and thermal damage by photothermal effects, the combination therapy group FG@HA + NIR demonstrated the strongest synergistic cell killing ability, laying the foundation for achieving excellent synergistic anti-tumor effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Distribution and synergistic anti-tumor effect in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The ideal nanodrug delivery system should effectively accumulate at the target site. To study the targeting ability and fluorescence tracing effect of NPs in vivo, a small animal in vivo imaging system was used to detect the distribution of NPs in 4T1 tumor-bearing mice (Fig. 7 A). At 6 h, the FG group reached the strongest fluorescence intensity at the tumor site (Fig. 7C), which then gradually decreased and was almost undetectable at 24 h. Meanwhile, the FG@HA group showed significantly higher accumulation at the tumor site compared to FG, reaching the highest accumulation at 12 h (Fig. 7C), and had a longer retention time, which might be related to the coating of HA, enhancing the targeting of NPs to the tumor site and prolonging the circulation time. Subsequently, at 24 h, the tumors and major organs from each group were dissected, and the fluorescence signal was observed and recorded (Fig. 7B and 7D). The FG group showed significant fluorescence at the tumor and kidney, which might be attributed to the accumulation of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs at the tumor and clearance through the kidney. The FG@HA group showed the strongest fluorescence at the tumor site, implying a large accumulation of FG@HA. Given the excellent synergistic cell killing ability and tumor accumulation effect of FG@HA, the tumor-bearing mouse model was constructed to investigate the in vivo antitumor ability of NPs. Based on the good photothermal conversion ability of FG@HA, the photothermal heating effect of NPs in vivo was first investigated. 12 h after administration, NIR was used to irradiate the tumor site of mice, and an infrared imaging device was used to monitor the temperature changes. As shown in Fig. S31, after 2 min of NIR irradiation, the temperature at the tumor site of the FG@HA group was significantly higher than normal saline group, reaching 43.2 °C. It had been reported that a local tumor temperature exceeding 42 °C could cause irreversible damage to tumor cells[16], which proved the potential of FG@HA for PTT. Subsequently, tumor-bearing mice were used to further study the in vivo antitumor effect of NPs, and the changes in the weight and tumor volume during treatment were recorded. As shown in Fig. S32, there was no significant decrease in the weight of the mice, implying the toxicity of the NPs could be negligible. Additionally, Fig. 7F showed that the tumor volume in the normal saline group continued to increase over the 15 days of treatment, while the tumor volume in the treatment groups increased slowly or even decreased. Among them, the FG@HA + NIR group showed the strongest tumor inhibition effect (82.3 % inhibition rate), which might be related to the large accumulation of NPs at the tumor site and the synergistic antitumor effect. At the end of the treatment, the tumors of the mice from each group were dissected and weighed (Fig. 7E and Fig. S33), and the tumor weight of the FG@HA + NIR group was the smallest, which also indicated that this group had the strongest antitumor ability. To further evaluate the apoptosis and ROS levels in the tumor tissue, H&E, DCF, and TUNEL staining were performed (Fig. 7G). In the normal saline group, the cell morphology was regular, the nuclei were intact, the ROS level was low, and apoptosis and necrosis of cells could be negligible. In contrast, in the FG@HA + NIR group, tumor cells showed disordered deformation, nuclear fragmentation, and significant green fluorescence in the DCF image, implying the generation of abundant ROS. Moreover, the TUNEL image showed the highest green fluorescence of apoptosis in the FG@HA + NIR group due to nuclear damage. Subsequently, H&E staining of the major organs from each group was performed to evaluate the potential biological toxicity of the NPs (Fig. S34), and no significant lesions were observed, indicating good biosafety of the NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The penetration of NPs in tumors
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the disadvantages of protein biologics, such as unstable physicochemical properties, high immunogenicity, and easy aggregation, it is difficult for them to penetrate deeply into tumor. The ideal protein drug delivery carrier should effectively penetrate the vascular wall and deliver the protein to the tumor tissue. Therefore, the penetration of NPs in tumor was further investigated, with CD31 used to mark tumor vasculature (Fig. 7H). In the Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs group, red fluorescence could be observed evenly distributed in the tumor tissue outside the vasculature. However, in the GOx group, the green fluorescence of GOx was almost entirely distributed within the vascular lumen, indicating that free GOx had difficulty penetrating from the vasculature into the tumor tissue. This might be related to the aggregation of GOx in vivo and the occurrence of immunogenic reactions. Surprisingly, in the FG group, both red and green fluorescence were almost entirely and similarly distributed outside the vascular lumen, showing co-localization in yellow. This might be related to the high expression of amino acid transporters in vascular endothelial cells [43,44], which promoted the transport of NPs from the vascular lumen to the outside, thereby enhancing the penetration of NPs at the tumor site. The NPs of the FG@HA group were distributed both inside and outside the vascular lumen. After NIR irradiation, the fluorescence inside the vascular lumen was almost undetectable, while the red and green fluorescence in the tumor tissue significantly increased, which might be related to the NIR irradiation promoting the depolymerization of the system and enhancing cellular uptake. The above results indicated that the formation of the FG complex significantly increased the accumulation of GOx from the vascular lumen to the tumor tissue and FG@HA + NIR had the strongest penetration effect.

In summary, the red-light CDs (Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs) with amino acid-like “edge” structure and unique FeN4 coordination “core” structure had been synthesized. Based on the excellent POD-like activity of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs, it was used in combination with GOx to form the FG cascade catalytic system, which was then encapsulated with HA to construct FG@HA. Under the stimulation of HAase/NIR, FG@HA could undergo complete depolymerization. Subsequently, the amino acid-like “edge” structure of Fe(Ⅲ)-CDs were exposed, effectively enhancing the transmembrane transport of GOx, allowing it to be efficiently taken up by tumor cells with high expression of ASCT2/LAT1 amino acid transporters. Simultaneously, the depolymerized nanosystem could expose the active sites of the nanozyme and initiate a cascade catalytic reaction to produce abundant ·OH. The photothermal effect enhanced catalytic activity in FG could intensify oxidative stress at the tumor site, ultimately inducing cell apoptosis. Cellular and animal experiments demonstrated that the formation of the FG complex enabled the previously difficult-to-transmembrane GOx to be efficiently taken up by tumor cells and enhanced its penetration in tumor. Encouragingly, in vivo antitumor experiments showed that FG@HA exhibited excellent photothermal synergistic therapy, with a tumor inhibition rate of 82.3 %, accompanied by good fluorescence tracing imaging effects. Notably, in this study, the structure of CDs nanozyme was disassembled into characteristic “edge” and catalytic “core” to further understand the cell uptake behavior and catalytic activity of CDs. This provided additional support for guiding the design of CDs nanozyme at the molecular level.
